problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem B ("Rapid Bushfire Response System"). Team 2127300 had to design a drone-based bushfire response system for Victoria's CFA using two drone types: SSA (Situational Awareness) drones that relay video/telemetry to front-line firefighters, and VHF/UHF radio-repeater drones that extend EOC-to-team radio range. Three tasks: (P1) determine the optimal number/mix of both drone types and the total purchase budget; (P2) adapt the model to the likelihood of extreme fires over the next decade and report the cost increase; (P3) determine optimal hovering locations for radio-repeater drones, accounting for terrain (mountains blocking VHF/UHF line-of-sight) and varying fire sizes.
models_used: Fire-severity scoring model: per-grid-cell evaluation value W_xy = P_xy * (k1*tem_xy + k2*bright_xy), with k1+k2=1, combining MODIS satellite fire frequency, temperature, and brightness into a single weighted index used as the optimization objective weight

Radio Wave Propagation Loss Model: free-space loss Los = 32.44 + 20*lg(d_km) + 20*lg(f_MHz) plus single-peak knife-edge diffraction loss via dimensionless parameter V = -h*sqrt((2/lambda)*(1/r1 + 1/r2)) and a 5-piece piecewise L(V) curve; used to derive repeater communication range (~18-18.5 km) from terrain/DEM

SSA drone duty-cycle / shift-coverage model: from flight endurance (2.5 h / 9000 s), charge time (1.75 h / 6300 s), travel times t_a=D_a/v, t_b=D_b/v, concludes 3 SSA drones per zone give uninterrupted (round-the-clock) coverage of a forward team

Genetic Algorithm (custom, roulette-wheel selection, two-point crossover, mutation) used twice: (a) to place N mobile EOCs maximizing covered fire-evaluation value sum, (b) to place radio-repeater drones minimizing count while maximizing covered fire value
Repeater-count heuristic tied to EOC distance: n_m = 2 if d_m <= 13.5 km else 3
SSA-count formula along wind axis: n_ssa = 3*l_max / r_ssa, where l_max is the longest fire projection on the 45-degree (NW, against prevailing SE wind) line within each EOC's control range
LSTM recurrent neural network to forecast daily max/min temperature for the next decade from 1938-2020 station data (with linear interpolation for missing values)
Exponential regression (MATLAB cftool) linking temperature to fire-site count: f(t) = 1.024 * (t/43.1)^13.89 * 2479, used to project a peak of 2698.14 fire sites (+8.84% vs source-data max)

Fire-spread simulation: scale the Jan 4 2020 fire map area by 1.0884 and probabilistically fill boundary grid cells to synthesize an extreme future fire map, then re-run the optimization (yields 27 repeaters / 48 SSA)
key_techniques: Grid/mesh discretization of Victoria from longitude/latitude DEM elevation data at 1 km sampling, converting a continuous geographic problem into a discrete optimization over cells (fits the 'discrete' problem type)
Anchoring on a worst-case day (Jan 4, 2020 = largest active fire area Oct 2019-Jul 2020) as the design reference, justifying a capacity-sizing decision

Reducing a hard joint problem via the assumption 'SSA observation range == front-line personnel mobility range', letting SSA drones stand in for forward teams and collapsing two coupled placement problems

Reusing one GA engine across sub-problems (EOC placement, repeater placement) and across P1->P3 by only adding constraints, e.g. P3 forces EOCs within 20 km of high mountains (x_h-x_e)^2+(y_h-y_e)^2 <= 20^2 and adds a terrain-dependent r_m schedule
Exploiting domain physics to prune complexity: drones fly at 4 km vs peaks under 2 km, so no obstacle-avoidance routing; DEM shows ~1-2 peaks per 20 km, so only single-peak diffraction needed
Encoding prevailing SE wind direction as a 45-degree projection axis to size SSA fleet against the worst-case fire front length

Two-stage forecasting chain: LSTM predicts the driver (temperature), then a fitted exponential maps the driver to the quantity of interest (fire count), then a spatial spread model turns the scalar back into a map the optimizer can consume

Sensitivity analysis by perturbing the one undisclosed parameter (drone flight altitude, +/-5% around 4 km) and reporting the small resulting change in repeater range (18.0 -> 17.2/18.7 km), demonstrating robustness
why_it_won: "From a judge's view, the paper wins on three things. First, real data and real physics: it pulls actual MODIS/Kaggle fire data and DEM elevation, picks a defensible worst-case day, and uses a textbook-cited (En Mu 1996) knife-edge diffraction loss model to turn terrain into concrete VHF/UHF range numbers rather than hand-waving. That grounding is rare and persuasive. Second, it answers the actual question end-to-end with hard deliverables: specific drone counts (24 repeaters + 30 SSA), a dollar budget (540,000 AUD), a quantified P2 cost delta (+210,000 AUD), and an explicit Table 3 of lat/lon coordinates for all 16 repeater locations in P3 - judges reward teams that produce the decision-ready artifact the prompt asked for. Third, model economy and reuse: one GA engine plus a small set of physically-justified simplifying assumptions let them cover all three problems coherently, and they layer a forecasting pipeline (LSTM -> exponential regression -> spatial spread) that looks sophisticated yet stays interpretable. The professional budget-request letter to the 'official' shows they read and served the communication requirement. Sensitivity analysis on the one assumed parameter plus an honest Strengths/Weaknesses section signals modeling maturity."
section_highlights: "Strong structure: tight per-problem Abstract with the headline numbers up front (counts, budgets, percentages); standard MCM skeleton (Intro/Restatement/Our Work, Assumptions with explicit Justification, a full Notations table, Models and Solutions, Sensitivity, Strengths/Weaknesses, Conclusions, References). Each problem follows a repeatable Analysis -> Visualization -> Model -> Solution rhythm, which makes a complex 3-part problem easy to follow. Heavy, purposeful use of figures: gridded fire heatmaps, 3D DEM surfaces, the GA fitness-convergence curve (Fig 10, shown to justify that the GA actually converged), the SSA shift-timing diagram (Fig 7), and a scatter of final repeater positions with coverage circles. The GA is written out as a clean 7-step recipe (feasible set -> encoding -> fitness -> roulette selection -> crossover -> mutation -> iteration). The Budget Request is delivered as an in-character letter to officials, directly satisfying the prompt's communication deliverable. Assumptions are each paired with a one-line justification rather than asserted bare."
pitfalls_or_limitations: P2 fire-spread model is crude: it scales the whole fire map uniformly by 1.0884 and fills boundary cells 'regardlessly' by proportion, ignoring spatial heterogeneity and real spread dynamics (the team admits this in Weaknesses)

Forecasting on thin data: the temperature->fire exponential regression is fit on only Aug 2019-Jan 2020 fire data; an exponent of 13.89 in f(t)=1.024*(t/43.1)^13.89*2479 is extremely steep and almost certainly overfit / not physically robust to extrapolation
Reported precision is unjustified: 2698.14 fire sites and 8-decimal-place lat/lon coordinates imply accuracy the underlying models cannot support

LSTM temperature forecast is asserted, not validated: Fig 13/14 show training loss but no held-out test error or forecast confidence interval, and a decade-ahead daily-temperature LSTM forecast is dubious

GA results are not stress-tested for optimality: no random-seed/multi-run comparison, no comparison against a baseline (greedy/set-cover), and only one fitness curve is shown, so 'global optimum' claims are unsupported

Numerous internal inconsistencies and typos that hurt credibility: average altitude stated as 'A=551.8 km' (should be ~551.8 m), V piecewise ranges overlap (0<=V<=0.8 and -1<=V<=0.8), section numbers misnumber (6.1 Strengths then 7.2 Weakness; two '7.' Conclusions), and the abstract's P2 totals (30k+18k increase) don't reconcile with the stated 210,000 AUD total
Sensitivity analysis is narrow: only flight altitude is perturbed; the influential weights k1/k2, the regression exponent, and GA hyperparameters are never varied

Key parameters chosen with weak justification: weighting coefficients k1,k2 are never actually solved or reported, and several drone specs (endurance, ranges) are taken from a single marketed product (Parrot Anafi)
reusable_patterns: Worst-case-day anchoring: for any capacity/sizing problem, select the extreme historical instance from real data as the design point and justify why (here, max active-fire-area day)
Composite weighted severity index over a grid (W = freq * weighted(physical signals)) to convert multi-channel sensor data into a single optimizer objective weight per cell

One reusable solver, many constraints: build a single GA (or generic optimizer) and adapt to new sub-problems purely by adding constraints (distance-to-mountain, terrain-dependent range) instead of rebuilding the model - efficient and coherent across a multi-part prompt

Physics-based feasibility pruning: state a quantitative inequality (cruise altitude >> peak height; peaks-per-km from DEM) to legitimately drop an entire modeling dimension (routing, multi-peak diffraction)

Driver-then-mapping forecasting chain: predict an underlying driver with a sequence model (LSTM on temperature), regress the target onto the driver, then re-spatialize the scalar prediction into a map the spatial optimizer can ingest

Duty-cycle coverage argument: from endurance + recharge + travel times, derive the minimum number of units for uninterrupted coverage (the '3 SSA drones per zone' shift diagram) - reusable for any continuous-coverage-with-recharge problem (sensors, patrols, satellites)
Deliver the decision artifact the prompt names: explicit counts, a line-item budget table, and a coordinate table - plus an in-character stakeholder letter to satisfy the communication requirement
Show convergence as evidence: include the GA fitness-vs-iteration curve so judges can see the heuristic actually settled, rather than just asserting optimality
Single-parameter sensitivity when one input is undisclosed: identify the assumption the problem left open (flight altitude), perturb it +/-5%, and report the bounded output change to argue stability
